narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 33.50, both of which are below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students in our dataset. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failing at 51.6%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. This decision hinged on a few key aspects of your academic record that carried the most weight in the prediction. Most importantly, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1st—meaning you placed in the bottom 10% of your classmates—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, as early struggles like this often signal challenges ahead. Your LSAT score, while respectable, worked in the opposite direction by pulling the prediction somewhat toward success, counterbalancing other concerns since it's a key indicator of legal reasoning potential. Next in influence was your Year 3 decile of 2nd, still in the lower ranks compared to the average around the middle of the pack, which further nudged the model toward expecting difficulties on the bar exam. Compared to typical students who pass, whose early and later deciles often land in higher brackets, your lower rankings in these areas set you apart as higher risk, even with your full-time commitment aligning with the norm for 93% of applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances succeed with targeted effort—but the model focuses on patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your early academic foundation through targeted coursework or tutoring if possible, and perhaps gaining practical experience like internships to build a more competitive profile. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your predicted odds significantly in your favor.
